data.title: OpenAI's Altman supports Nvidia's Huang on importance of open models in AI race
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) CEO Sam Altman agreed with Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang that open models are important to help the U.S. win the artificial intelligence race.

"I want the US to win in AI both in open-source and proprietary models, and I am glad to see this," Altman posted on X on Friday in response to Huang.

Huang posted earlier in the day, his first post ever on the platform, sharing a signed letter sent to the U.S. Congress on open AI models and the need for American leadership in the space.

The three-page letter was signed by Nvidia, along with other tech industry heavyweights, including Meta Platforms (META), Box (BOX), CrowdStrike (CRWD), Microsoft (MSFT), and others. Twenty-five entities in total signed the letter.

"Open weights expand access to the AI economy," the letter reads. "Startups, established businesses, universities, and public institutions can build on advanced models without training one from scratch or paying frontier-model prices for every task … America wins the AI era by diffusing it into the workflows of factories, hospitals, farms, classrooms, and Main Street businesses."

"Open weights also strengthen competition, and competition is what keeps the gains of AI broadly shared rather than concentrated in a few hands," it added.

data.title: Charter targets flat 3.5x leverage within 3 years as it expects standalone 2026 EBITDA to decline around 1%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Charter Communications (CHTR) Q2 2026

Management view

  • Christopher Winfrey (President, CEO & Director) highlighted mobile momentum, saying, "During the second quarter, we added over 400,000 Spectrum Mobile lines" and that Charter now has "over 12.5 million mobile lines" and "remain[s] the fastest-growing mobile provider in our footprint."
  • Winfrey framed broadband pressure as primarily acquisition-driven, noting, "Our second quarter Internet customer loss of 172,000 was higher than a year ago, similar to what we saw in the first quarter," while also stating, "Softer gross additions remains the primary driver of our Internet customer growth weakness, while churn remained largely unchanged."
  • Winfrey announced a leadership change tied to operations and go-to-market execution: "On September 1, Nick Jeffery will join as Chief Operating Officer."
  • Winfrey updated Cox timing and integration posture: "We're now hoping to close in mid- to late August," and reiterated synergy expectations: "We still expect run rate transaction expense synergies of at least $800 million per year. And while we'll update that estimate after close, I think it will grow to $1 billion."
  • Winfrey disclosed a capital allocation change: "Today, we're moving our post-transaction leverage target to a flat 3.5x, which we expect to achieve within 3 years following the close of the Cox and Liberty Broadband transactions."
  • Jessica Fischer (Chief Financial Officer) said Cox transition expenses were $65 million and added, "Transition expenses have been coming in a bit higher than expected."

Outlook

  • Fischer guided, "Currently, for the full year 2026, we expect standalone Charter EBITDA, excluding the impact of transition costs to decline around 1% year-over-year."
  • Fischer pointed to second-half supports, saying, "The back half of this year will benefit from political advertising, cost pass-throughs and efficiency initiatives," while also stating Charter is "working on a number of additional initiatives to improve the full year trajectory."
  • On broadband pricing dynamics into Q3, Fischer said, "Broadband ARPU will improve sequentially in Q3," and Winfrey added, "you're going to have lift coming from that going away and in addition to that, the rate increase pass-through."
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management shifted from targeting a post-transaction leverage range to a single point. In Q1, Fischer said Charter would "target the low end of the 3.5 to 3.75x range" after close; in Q2, Winfrey said the target is now "a flat 3.5x."

Financial results

  • Fischer reported, "consolidated second quarter revenue was down by 1.7% year-over-year," and "Adjusted EBITDA declined by 4.3% year-over-year in the quarter and declined by 3.2% when excluding transition expenses."
  • Fischer reported profitability and cash flow, saying, "We generated $1.3 billion of net income attributable to Charter shareholders in the second quarter," and "Second quarter free cash totaled $1 billion."
  • Fischer reiterated full-year CapEx expectations and longer-term trajectory: "For standalone Charter, we continue to expect total 2026 capital expenditures to reach approximately $11.4 billion," and "our run rate capital expenditures for standalone Charter would be below $8 billion per year" after evolution and expansion initiatives conclude.
  • On capital returns and leverage snapshot, Fischer said Charter "repurchased 4 million Charter shares totaling $838 million at an average price of $210 per share" and ended Q2 at "4.18x" net leverage ("4.21x pro forma for the pending Liberty Broadband transaction").

Q&A

  • Craig Moffett, MoffettNathanson LLC: asked for an update on broadband ARPU and Charter’s wireless offload; Jessica Fischer (Chief Financial Officer): replied, "Broadband ARPU will improve sequentially in Q3" and emphasized Charter focuses on "connectivity ARPU and overall customer relationship ARPU excluding the programmer app allocation"; Christopher Winfrey (President, CEO & Director): said wireless offload had been "at 88%" but a product change "pushed us back down to 87%" due to "more 5G traffic usage."
  • Vikash Harlalka, New Street Research LLP: asked what drove the lowered EBITDA target and sought comment on Starlink partnership reports; Fischer: cited weaker "broadband subscribers and ARPU" assumptions and pressure in "fuel and medical" while pointing to actions including "simplification on the overhead side"; Winfrey: said, "I want to be clear" the company is "targeting to do better," and on Starlink stated, "it's natural for us" to talk to "many industry players" but "there's something to announce or talk about... that certainly is not the case today."
  • Steven Cahall, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked about potential wireless/network-build partnerships and Cox trend differences; Winfrey: discussed selective wholesale opportunities (citing "the Amazon deal") and said Cox subscriber and revenue trends have been "a couple of clicks lower than here at Spectrum" with "No change to the playbook."
  • Walter Piecyk, LightShed Partners, LLC: pressed on funding a "network build" and the composition of offload; Winfrey: said, "We don't have any plans to do anything different as it relates to our CapEx trajectory," and described the approach as "a capital-light approach" with no need to "go after that last 12%" given MVNO partnerships and WiFi/CBRS economics.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative, pressing on EBITDA target changes, broadband ARPU sensitivity, and Starlink/wireless strategy; questions repeatedly probed competitive positioning and whether Charter might need to change strategy.
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks (free cash flow and CapEx reduction confidence), and more defensive/clarifying in Q&A, using phrases such as Winfrey’s "I want to be clear" and emphasizing no near-term change to CapEx plans.
  • Versus Q1, Q2 carried more pointed questioning on downgraded EBITDA expectations and on wireless/satellite narratives, while management introduced a more explicit balance-sheet adjustment ("flat 3.5x") and a near-term buyback pause.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q2 shifted Cox timing from Q1’s "summer close" expectation to Winfrey’s "mid- to late August" target.
  • Q2 updated the EBITDA outlook to Fischer’s "decline around 1%" for 2026, compared with Q1’s plan (excluding transition costs) to "grow EBITDA slightly this year" driven by political advertising.
  • Q2 tightened the leverage framework to "a flat 3.5x" target, versus Q1’s post-close "3.5 to 3.75x" range.
  • Analysts’ focus moved from broader cable M&A/regulatory framing in Q1 toward ARPU mechanics, wireless offload, and Starlink-related speculation in Q2.

Risks and concerns

  • Fischer said Charter continues to see "expanded fixed wireless competition" along with "ongoing mobile substitution" and "fiber overlap growth" with "aggressive promotions by certain competitors."
  • Fischer added, "we haven't observed meaningful share loss to Starlink" but said Charter continues to "monitor it closely and take it seriously."
  • Fischer flagged execution risk around integration planning costs: "Transition expenses have been coming in a bit higher than expected," while maintaining, "we still expect the sum of our Cox transition costs and capital expenditures to be at or better than what we anticipated."

Final takeaway

Management emphasized fast mobile line growth and improved video losses, while describing broadband weakness as a gross-adds problem amid fixed wireless and fiber competition. The biggest strategic updates were a planned mid- to late August Cox close, confidence in at least $800 million of run-rate expense synergies (with Winfrey saying he thinks it will grow to $1 billion), and a move to a flat 3.5x post-transaction leverage target within three years, paired with a temporary pause in buybacks through Q3 and a stated expectation to restart repurchases in Q4.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: First Bank targets $200M loan growth as pipelines hold near $323M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Bank (FRBA) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Patrick Ryan framed Q2 as a rebound, saying, "Q2 was a much better quarter" and pointing to renewed balance sheet momentum: loans grew $68 million and deposits grew $96 million, while "Our margin held in at 3.68%." He added that, "Absent abnormal payoff activity between now and the end of the year, we believe we should be able to meet or exceed our $200 million loan growth goal for the year," while also targeting deposit growth of $200 million.

  • Ryan said credit costs normalized as "Our provision for credit losses came down significantly to more normalized levels as the profile within our small business portfolio showed signs of improvement," while noting one new issue: "Our non-performing asset ratio did tick up as one CRE loan moved to non-accrual." He added, "we're comfortable where we stand with the loan given collateral and guarantor support."

  • CFO Andrew Hibshman reported profitability improvement, stating, "we recorded net income of $10.9 million or $0.43 per diluted share," and said credit was the key driver: "A $3.3 million decline in credit loss expense compared to the first quarter drove improvement to our bottom line." Hibshman also highlighted capital actions: "we executed share repurchases totaling about 325,000 shares during the quarter."

  • Chief Lending Officer Peter Cahill described a stronger production environment, saying, "New loans closed and funded in the second quarter totaled $174 million, up 64% from the first quarter," and emphasized mix shift: "C&I and owner-occupied real estate made up 61% of our new loans during the first half of the year."

Outlook

  • Management linked second-half expectations to lending and funding pipelines. CEO Patrick Ryan said, "Pipelines are strong," and reiterated, "we believe we should be able to meet or exceed our $200 million loan growth goal for the year," alongside a plan "to meet or exceed that $200 million deposit growth goal for the year."

  • On margin direction, Ryan said, "we're sort of targeting flat to down slightly on the margin as we move through this year," and added, "we don't see it declining more than 1 basis point or 2 as we move forward," citing higher funding costs alongside improving market rates.

  • CFO Andrew Hibshman cautioned, "We expect continued declines in acquisition accounting accretion over the next several quarters," and said funding remains competitive: "the ongoing competitive landscape for core deposits remains challenging."

Financial Results

  • CFO Andrew Hibshman reported second-quarter profitability and returns, stating, "we recorded net income of $10.9 million or $0.43 per diluted share, which translates to a 1.09% return on average assets," and added that "Year-over-year, EPS grew 6.6%."

  • Hibshman said core spread metrics were stable: "our net interest margin was 3.68%, down just 1 basis point from the linked quarter," while noting operating discipline: "Our continued focus on tight expense management produced a 54.5% efficiency ratio and marked our 28th consecutive quarter of operating with a sub-60% efficiency ratio."

  • On fee and expense lines, Hibshman reported, "Non-interest income was $2.1 million," and "Non-interest expenses were $20.1 million," adding that management believes it can "continue to drive growth without adding to the expense base" and expects the effective tax rate to be "approximately 25%."

Q&A

  • Bader Hijleh, Piper Sandler & Co.: asked about deposit seasonality and mix; CEO Patrick Ryan said, "we generally see a decline, especially in the noninterest-bearing in Q1" and "we start to see some money coming back during the second and third quarters," adding the bank will stay in-market to "win our share of the new deposit business."

  • Bader Hijleh, Piper Sandler & Co.: asked about deposit pricing and spot competition; CEO Ryan said, "we're seeing the pricing anywhere from 4% to 4.10%, 4.15%, 4.25% in some cases," and tied margin expectations to rate moves, saying, "the good news is the 5 and the 10-year treasuries and the FHLB rates are moving higher."

  • Bader Hijleh, Piper Sandler & Co.: asked if the Q2 expense run rate is sustainable; Ryan said, "Q2 is a much more sort of base run rate," and CFO Andrew Hibshman added, "there wasn't really any kind of unusual noise" and "we should be able to manage expenses pretty flat and continue to grow."

  • Emily Graves, Hovde Group: asked about new commercial origination rates; CEO Ryan said the range had been "between... 6.5% and 7%," and CLO Peter Cahill added, "the weighted average new loan rate was between... 6.50% to 6.75%."

  • Emily Graves, Hovde Group: asked about the new CRE non-accrual and loss expectations; Ryan said it was "office space" and "underperforming," but added, "we have strong guarantor support" and "we expect full recovery there."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly negative, concentrating questions on deposit competition, deposit pricing spot rates, sustainability of expenses, new-loan pricing, and loss expectations around a new CRE non-accrual.

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and measured in Q&A, emphasizing improved momentum and controllable items, while acknowledging competition: "the competition for deposits in our markets remains high" (CEO Patrick Ryan).

  • Versus Q1, management language was more upbeat in Q2 ("much better quarter") compared with Q1’s "Earnings came in below our expectations," while analyst focus shifted from the small business credit-scored portfolio in Q1 to funding costs, origination pricing, and the CRE non-accrual in Q2.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 messaging emphasized normalized credit and accelerating growth, with CEO Patrick Ryan saying loan growth "feels sustainable" and forecasting the ability to "meet or exceed" the $200 million loan growth goal; Q1 centered on remediation after "Elevated credit costs in the credit scored small business portfolio" and unusually high payoffs.

  • Loan-production commentary strengthened in Q2, with CLO Peter Cahill highlighting $174 million in closings and "probable fundings" of $323 million; in Q1, Cahill cited $106 million in closings and a pipeline of $383 million, with greater emphasis on payoff-driven headwinds.

  • Management participants changed in prepared remarks: Q1 included retail banking commentary from EVP Darleen Gillespie, while Q2 deposit discussion was led by CFO Andrew Hibshman.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged continued funding pressure, with CEO Patrick Ryan stating, "the competition for deposits in our markets remains high," and CFO Andrew Hibshman warning that "the ongoing competitive landscape for core deposits remains challenging."

  • Credit risk discussion centered on two areas: the credit-scored small business portfolio (with management pointing to improvement) and a new non-accrual office CRE loan; Ryan said it was "underperforming" but emphasized collateral and "strong guarantor support" with an expectation of "full recovery."

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q2 as a return to stronger growth and more normalized credit costs, anchored by loan and deposit expansion, a stable net interest margin, and an expense posture management described as a sustainable run rate. Executives reiterated plans to reach (or exceed) $200 million in both loan and deposit growth for 2026, while acknowledging elevated deposit competition and pointing to a strong loan pipeline, continued C&I mix shift, and a targeted full recovery on the quarter’s new non-accrual CRE credit.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Customers Bancorp targets low-40s efficiency ratio run rate in 2027 as cubiX real estate payments aims for 20% of units
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Customers Bancorp (CUBI) Q2 2026

Management view

  • “Total loans grew 4% in the quarter and 17% year-over-year to a record $18 billion,” “total deposits grew over $140 million to a record $21.7 billion,” and “noninterest-bearing deposits hit a second consecutive record at $6.9 billion or 32% of total deposits,” Samvir Sidhu (President, CEO & Vice Chairman) said, also citing “pristine credit quality and robust capital levels” alongside “modestly buying back shares.”

  • On AI strategy and expected operating impact, Sidhu said, “we announced a strategic collaboration with OpenAI,” and described a pilot where “we … successfully closed C&I and CRE loans that utilize this underwriting engine within a week,” adding, “we’ve set a goal of getting to a low 40s run rate efficiency ratio in 2027 versus the 50% or so we’re at today.”

  • On payments, Sidhu said the real estate payments vertical is scaling quickly, noting “transaction volume is up roughly 7x,” “spot deposit balances are up more than 4x, reaching $400 million,” and “we surpassed $5 trillion in cumulative transaction activity,” while also changing his prior stance: “At the end of last year, I told you we did not expect this to be a growth vertical. However… we now expect cubiX to be a growth area in 2027.”

  • Mark McCollom (Executive VP & CFO) said, “We delivered EPS of $2.05,” and added, “Net interest income was over $193 million,” while “our second quarter net interest margin of 3.17% is expected to be the low point for 2026.”

Outlook

  • “We expect our net interest margin to move back toward first quarter levels in the third quarter and to build from there,” McCollom said, adding, “We also expect net interest income to be stronger in the back half of the year.”

  • “We are reaffirming all key metrics,” McCollom said, tying the reaffirmation to drivers including “the account and balance momentum from our new teams and real estate payments vertical,” and stating that “the combination of loan and deposit growth opportunities should result in solid growth in net interest income.”

  • Versus Q1 2026 messaging, Q2 emphasized a defined turning point: “We view the second quarter as the inflection point for the year,” and reiterated what was previously signaled: “as we signaled last quarter, our second quarter net interest margin of 3.17% is expected to be the low point for 2026,” McCollom said.

Financial results

  • “Net interest income was over $193 million, up $16 million or 9% year-over-year,” McCollom said, and added, “On a linked quarter annualized basis, net interest income grew about 4%.”

  • “Noninterest expense was $114.9 million in the quarter, which included about $1 million of severance,” McCollom said, adding, “our noninterest expense as a percent of average assets was 1.82%.”

  • On cost-out execution, McCollom said, “we have now achieved the full $30 million run rate target,” with “about $4 million” from “revenue initiatives” and “about $26 million” from “expense initiatives.”

  • On capital and credit, McCollom said, “Our CET1 ratio was 12.8%,” and “credit quality remained stable across the board,” with “commercial charge-offs… low at just 18 basis points” and “Reserve coverage… 293%.”

Q&A

  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: asked timing for the real estate payments goal of “about 20% of payments”; President Sidhu: “that is a 2027 goal.”

  • Moss, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: asked about marginal deposit costs and cadence of funding-cost declines; President Sidhu: “the teams that we’re recruiting are bringing in… noninterest-bearing deposits,” and “we remixed about $600 million or so of higher cost funding.”

  • Kelly Motta, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: asked about lower average cash balances and whether tied to cubiX/DA; President Sidhu: “DA trading was down in the second quarter,” and “DA balances were $3.8 billion, but total cubiX balances were roughly flat in the quarter.”

  • Motta, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: asked what supports a back-half NII ramp; CFO McCollom: “the exit point at June 30… plus actual loan balances… gives us confidence for the back half of the year.”

  • Michael Pietrini, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked how much of the real estate pipeline converts in 2026; President Sidhu: “our internal target is getting that to about $1.5 billion by the end of the year.”

  • Pietrini, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked for quantified AI savings to date; President Sidhu: “we’re seeing productivity lifts today,” but “our focus is decoupling our expense base from our revenue growth as we get into 2027.”

  • Brian Wilczynski, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: asked if loan growth is skewing to the high end of guidance; CFO McCollom: “the higher end of the range would be more likely.”

  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen, Research Division: asked about the CLARITY Act; President Sidhu: “we’re very, very supportive of market structure and clarity,” and said agency rulemaking “should hopefully provide structure.”

  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: asked for a June NIM “jumping off point”; CFO McCollom: “we don’t usually talk about monthly results,” and reiterated, “our third quarter NIM is going to be closer to our first quarter net interest margin.”

  • Navas, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: asked when conservatism on deploying DA balances could shift; President Sidhu: “in the next quarter or 2, I’ll be able to sort of give some more confidence,” and “2027 should be a growth year for cubiX related deposits.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive and detail-oriented, with supportive framing (e.g., “Nice nice quarter here,” Moss, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division) while repeatedly pressing on cubiX balances, NII/NIM inflection timing, and deployability of digital-asset funds.

  • Management’s tone was confident in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, repeatedly using reaffirmation language such as “we are reaffirming all key metrics” (Executive VP & CFO McCollom) and forward-looking conviction such as “we intend to be the one that proves what serious adoption looks like” (President, CEO & Vice Chairman Sidhu).

  • Versus Q1 2026, the tone around cubiX shifted from diversification-building to explicit growth expectations, with Sidhu now stating “we now expect cubiX to be a growth area in 2027,” while Q&A focus remained consistent on balances, funding costs, and NII trajectory.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q2 introduced a clearer payments growth pivot and timeline, with Sidhu moving from earlier caution to “we now expect cubiX to be a growth area in 2027,” alongside a defined target that the real estate payments vertical “could represent 20% of all payment units” as a “2027 goal.”

  • Q2 added more explicit NIM path language, with McCollom calling Q2 “the inflection point for the year” and the “low point for 2026,” while Q1 focused more on reaffirming full-year NII growth expectations and balance-sheet leverage.

  • Analyst questioning in Q2 concentrated more on DA trading-driven balance variability, real estate vertical conversion, and the “jumping off point” for NIM; Q1 questions leaned more toward cubiX mix/expansion, AI revenue opportunities, and written agreement progress.

Risks and concerns

  • Management flagged activity sensitivity in digital assets, with Sidhu saying, “DA trading was down in the second quarter,” and linking it to “lower payments float,” while emphasizing offsetting growth: “total cubiX balances were roughly flat in the quarter.”

  • Management highlighted that AI financial benefits are still building, with Sidhu saying, “some of the larger lifts actually take quarters, not weeks,” and framing near-term focus as “decoupling our expense base from our revenue growth as we get into 2027.”

  • Management emphasized near-term uncertainty around deployability of DA balances, with Sidhu saying, “we… have continued to be conservative,” but added, “in the next quarter or 2, I’ll be able to sort of give some more confidence.”

Final takeaway

Customers Bancorp’s Q2 2026 call emphasized continued balance-sheet expansion alongside a sharper 2027 framing: management reiterated its full-year targets while describing Q2 as the year’s NIM inflection and low point, outlined a 2027 efficiency ambition in the “low 40s,” and positioned cubiX as a 2027 growth area driven by scaling real estate payments and broader vertical expansion, with analysts focusing most on the durability of cubiX balances, the NII/NIM ramp mechanics into the back half, and when digital-asset-related funds could become more deployable.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SS&C record Q2 ignites 11% rally: Outlook upgrade, buybacks steal the show
data.description:
SS&C Technologies (SSNC) shares jumped over ~11% to ~$74.41 on Friday after the company reported better-than-expected second-quarter results and highlighted record profitability and shareholder returns.

Adjusted EPS came in at $1.76, beating estimates by $0.08, while revenue rose 10.3% YoY to $1.70B, ahead of expectations by $30M.

The company also reported a record $670.7M in adjusted EBITDA, with margin expanding to 39.5%, while adjusted organic revenue grew 7.6%.

The company also generated $716.4M in operating cash flow during the first six months of the year, up 11.1% from the same period last year.

Importantly, investors cheered the company's stronger outlook. SS&C raised its FY2026 guidance, now expecting adjusted revenue of $6.67B-$6.83B and adjusted EPS of $6.93-$7.25. Management also said it is targeting a 40% adjusted EBITDA margin in the fourth quarter.

The company also returned $499.2M to shareholders during the quarter, including a record $435.2M in share buybacks and $64M in dividends.

During the earnings call, CEO Bill Stone said buybacks remain SS&C's top capital allocation priority and described the quarter as "record-setting," driven by strong sales, multiyear renewals, and healthy market activity.

Management also struck an upbeat tone for the rest of the year, saying its pipeline is "full of opportunities" and that the company has "opportunities to outperform."

Despite today's sharp rally, SS&C shares are still down 15.31% so far this year, compared with an 8.22% gain for the S&P 500 (SP500).

data.title: Kinsale outlines $337M buyback authorization as commercial property volume shrinks in competitive market
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kinsale Capital Group (KNSL) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Michael Kehoe (Chairman of the Board, CEO & President) framed results alongside a tougher pricing backdrop: "Gross written premium was down 5% and net written premium was down 1.4%, and net earned premium was up 8.9% for the quarter. Kinsale's combined ratio was 75.5% for the quarter."
  • Kehoe said competitive pressure remained concentrated in large layered commercial property: "It's definitely a buyer's market. And as a consequence, we are writing a shrinking volume of business in that specific market."
  • Kehoe highlighted capital return as a key action: "Last night, we announced an expansion of our buyback authorization to include an additional $250 million, bringing our current authorization to $337 million."
  • Bryan Petrucelli (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer) emphasized underwriting and investment income momentum: "Net income and net operating earnings increased by 31.1% and 13.3%, respectively, quarter-over-quarter."
  • Stuart Winston (Executive VP & Chief Underwriting Officer) reiterated underwriting discipline through the soft market: "our approach to soft markets like commercial property, construction or certain professional lines is not to chase the market down, but to continue to price business in a way that meets our return thresholds and to be opportunistic where it makes sense."

Outlook

  • Management did not provide explicit EPS, revenue, premium, or combined ratio guidance in the prepared remarks or Q&A.
  • Kehoe described operating targets as return-threshold driven rather than volume driven: "Well, we manage everything to a 20% ROE or greater, and I think most of our product lines are probably running ahead of that."
  • Petrucelli pointed investors to Q2 as a baseline for expenses following the June 1 reinsurance renewal: "I think if you look in aggregate, the second quarter expense ratio should be a good measure for you going forward."
  • Compared with Q1, management’s outlook language stayed centered on profitability-first and selectivity in commercial property, while highlighting that year-over-year comparisons should ease as prior-year commercial property volume rolls off.

Financial Results

  • Petrucelli reported operating EPS and key underwriting items: "diluted operating earnings per share continues to improve. and was $5.54 per share for the quarter" and "The 75.5% combined ratio for the quarter included 4.5 points from net favorable prior year loss reserve development" with "1.3 points in cat losses."
  • Petrucelli attributed the higher expense ratio to reinsurance structure while stressing operating efficiency: "We produced a 21.7% expense ratio" and "The other underwriting expense portion of this ratio... was 10.3% for the quarter."
  • On investment earnings and balance-sheet float, Petrucelli said: "net investment income increased by 19.9%" and "Kinsale's float... grew to $3.4 billion" with "New money yields... around 5.25%" and "an average duration of 4.25 years."

Q&A

  • Pablo Singzon, JPMorgan Chase & Co: "The current year accident loss ratio improved from 2Q '25"; Salmaan Allibhai (Senior VP & Chief Actuary) responded, "losses for the quarter came in below expectations as they have for the last few quarters" and "our reserves are as conservative as they've ever been in our company's history."
  • Singzon, JPMorgan Chase & Co: asked about reinsurance commissions; Petrucelli answered, "we renewed our reinsurance treaties on June 1st" and "the second quarter expense ratio should be a good measure for you going forward."
  • Hristian Getsov, Wells Fargo: asked about ROE pressure as E&S casualty softens; Kehoe replied, "we manage everything to a 20% ROE or greater" and referenced "24.4% for the quarter."
  • Getsov, Wells Fargo: asked about broker commissions in a softer market; Kehoe said, "brokers are critical to our success" and "we offer our brokers the best customer service in the industry."
  • Daniel Cohen, BMO: asked whether commercial property is at a trough and the outlook for 2027; Winston responded, "there's no telling on where it's going to go in 2027" and "if we shrink because of it, it is what it is in that division."
  • Andrew Andersen, Jefferies: asked about "increase in inbound accounts"; Winston said, "It's a little bit of both" and cited "our idle broker initiative" while adding, "we're just seeing more accounts buying."
  • Andersen, Jefferies: asked about social inflation and casualty trends; Allibhai said, "For casualty loss cost trends, we'd say probably mid-single digits" and "we're just not seeing a whole lot of it."
  • Mark Hughes, Truist: asked about property equilibrium and buyback pacing; Kehoe said, "eventually, we're going to get to some equilibrium" and on buybacks, "We're going to continue to lean into it."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly negative, with repeated probing on accident-year loss ratio drivers, casualty/social inflation, and when commercial property pricing stabilizes, including questions like "Does Kinsale view this 2Q as maybe the trough there" and "when or if we can get to a point of some kind of equilibrium."
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and steady, repeatedly emphasizing discipline and reserving conservatism, including Allibhai’s "There's really nothing out of the ordinary" and Kehoe’s "we're confident in our model."
  • Versus Q1, the tone around soft-market pressure was consistent, while Q2 added stronger emphasis on repurchases as an outlet for capital during slower growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 highlighted deeper commercial property contraction and explicitly called it "a buyer's market," while Q1 described "an abundance of competition and falling rates" in the same division.
  • Q2 introduced a major capital allocation update with the expanded repurchase authorization, while Q1 discussion focused more on disclosure additions (premium by underwriting division) and AI rollout.
  • Analysts maintained similar themes across both quarters (pricing pressure, loss ratio dynamics, reinsurance/commissions), with Q2 more focused on whether property has bottomed and how sustainable the loss ratio is as mix shifts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management described market-driven pressure in large layered commercial property: "material rate declines combined with expanding coverage" and "we are writing a shrinking volume of business in that specific market."
  • Winston flagged soft-market behavior as a profitability risk: "Growth driven by compromised profit margins is easy to manufacture, but costly to unwind," and said Kinsale will "stay disciplined to our underwriting standards."
  • Petrucelli noted higher net commission ratio pressure tied to reinsurance retentions, while stating the trade is intended to be economically favorable: "The overall expense ratio increase is attributable to a higher net commission ratio resulting from higher reinsurance retentions."

Final Takeaway

Kinsale management characterized Q2 as a period of strong profitability alongside competitive E&S conditions, with heightened pressure in large shared and layered commercial property driving reduced volume in that division, while other lines and smaller-account areas supported activity. Executives repeatedly emphasized underwriting discipline and conservative reserves, pointed to Q2 expense structure as a forward baseline after the June 1 reinsurance renewal, and expanded share repurchase capacity by adding $250 million to bring total authorization to $337 million.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ameris projects mid-single-digit 2026 loan and deposit growth while expanding into Nashville
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ameris Bancorp (ABCB) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Core fundamentals at Ameris remained strong in the second quarter" with "adjusted ROA of 1.53%, adjusted PPNR ROA of 2.24% and adjusted return on TCE of over 14%" (Vice Chairman & CEO H. Proctor).
  • "Our loan production was $2.4 billion in the second quarter" and "our loan pipeline remained robust at $2.7 billion" (Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor).
  • "Reported expenses were impacted by an $82.5 million litigation accrual related to a jury verdict in an employment case in California" and "despite our planned appeal, we accrued the full amount of the verdict plus related costs this quarter" (Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor).
  • "We reported net income of $51.4 million or $0.77 per diluted share" and "adjusted net income of $107.3 million or $1.60 per diluted share when you exclude the litigation accrual and the Visa B and BOLI gains" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Nicole Stokes).
  • "As we recently announced, we are also excited to be expanding Ameris' footprint into the attractive Nashville, Tennessee market" and "we're glad to have found a solid team of Nashville-focused bankers that have joined our growing franchise" (Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor).

Outlook

  • "We do anticipate some slight margin compression over the next few quarters due to higher deposit costs to fund our balance sheet growth" and "we believe the margin could decline just a few basis points per quarter over the next couple of quarters" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes).
  • "We project loan and deposit growth in the mid-single-digit range for the year" and "we expect that longer-term deposit growth will be the governor of our loan growth" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes).
  • "We continue to anticipate net charge-offs in that 20 to 25 basis point range for the remainder of 2026" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes).
  • Versus last quarter’s margin framing, management again pointed to modest pressure: "we do anticipate some slight margin compression over the next few quarters" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes, Q2) compared to "we do anticipate we could have some slight margin compression over the next few quarters" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes, Q1).

Financial Results

  • "Our net interest margin was stable this quarter at 3.88%" with "a 4 basis point positive impact from higher asset yields, exactly offsetting the increase in funding costs" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes).
  • "We ended the quarter with $28.5 billion of total assets compared to $28.1 billion last quarter" and "loans grew $349.9 million or about 6% annualized" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes).
  • "Ending deposits down about $49 million" while "our quarterly average balance grew over $240 million or about 4.4% annualized" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes).
  • "During the second quarter, we recorded $17.3 million of provision expense" and "annualized net charge-offs decreased to 20 basis points" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes).
  • "We repurchased about 226,600 shares at an average price of $83.71 per share" and "our remaining share repurchase authorization was $65.4 million at the end of the second quarter" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes).

Q&A

  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: asked if higher bond yields were sustainable; Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes said "we did have a bump of about 40 basis points in our bond yield" tied to "some tips, some inflation bonds" and added "those bond yields should come back down just a little bit going forward."
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: asked about new loan pricing momentum; Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes said CRE payoffs were "about 5.04%" versus "total company production this quarter of 6.20%" and "the core bank came on at 6.39%."
  • Christopher Marinac, Brean: asked about reserves and whether the reserve approach could change; Corporate Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Douglas Strange said "we continue to be model-driven with our Moody's" and "we've primarily gone to a 50-50 weighting" with the reserve at "1.62%" and "if you add the unfunded, it's 1.86%."
  • Christopher Marinac, Brean: asked about sustaining low charge-offs; Corporate Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Strange said "for the year, we are providing guidance of 20 to 25 basis points" and Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor added, "Correct" when asked about consistency beyond this year.
  • Christopher Marinac, Brean: asked about buyback pace; Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes said "I don't think you're going to see as aggressive it was what you saw in the first quarter" and Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor said, "we'll just remain opportunistic with that."
  • Jake Morton, Stephens: asked about loan growth by asset class and geography; Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor said "we are probably more encouraged now than we've been in long term" and pipelines are "across our entire Southeastern footprint" while reiterating "we feel very confident in our mid-single-digit estimates" with funding as a constraint.
  • Jake Morton, Stephens: asked about brokered deposit growth; Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes said it was "really an offset of the seasonal public funds" and added Ameris used brokered to "backfill" rather than "compete on some of those hot deposits."
  • Zita Lopez Wong, D.A. Davidson: asked about securities yield and reinvestment; Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes said a bond-book "onetime" added "about 3 basis points of margin" and noted "about $240 million" of maturities in Q3 "in the low 4s" that would reprice "up between 75 and 90 basis points."
  • Jackson Andrew, Piper Sandler: asked about mortgage outlook; Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor said production "remains consistent" but "until we see a little relief" in rates, "I don't think we'll get the incremental lift" expected in 2H.
  • Timothy Mitchell, Raymond James: asked about capital deployment beyond buybacks; Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor said the "priority stack has not changed" with "organic growth first" and M&A requiring "something pretty special."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-slightly negative on margin durability and funding pressure, probing one-time bond impacts and deposit competition, including "Margin would have actually declined had we not had that" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes, responding to KBW).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautionary on funding-driven margin pressure in Q&A, including "we do anticipate some slight margin compression" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes) alongside "we are probably more encouraged now than we've been in long term" (Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor).
  • Versus Q1, management repeated similar confidence on core profitability and growth but introduced a major one-time legal item: "Reported expenses were impacted by an $82.5 million litigation accrual" (Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor, Q2) compared with Q1’s emphasis on buybacks and operating leverage without a comparable charge.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 introduced the litigation accrual, which management highlighted as a driver of reported expenses: "impacted by an $82.5 million litigation accrual" (Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor), while Q1 focused on being "notably active" in buybacks and reported earnings without such an item (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes, Q1).
  • Margin commentary stayed consistent, with Q2 saying "margin compression" could be "just a few basis points per quarter" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes) versus Q1’s "few basis points per quarter" and "5 to 10 total basis points lower over the next few quarters" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes, Q1).
  • Capital return messaging shifted from Q1’s "highest level of buybacks" (Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor, Q1) to Q2’s expectation that repurchases would be less aggressive: "I don't think you're going to see as aggressive" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes).
  • Strategic footprint expanded in Q2 with Nashville: "expanding Ameris' footprint into the attractive Nashville, Tennessee market" (Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor); Q1 emphasized leveraging "disruption in our Southeastern footprint" within existing overlap markets (Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor, Q1).

Risks and Concerns

  • Litigation uncertainty was the largest discrete issue: "we accrued the full amount of the verdict plus related costs" and "with this being ongoing litigation, we are unable to comment any further on that" (Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor).
  • Funding costs and deposit competition were framed as a near-term earnings headwind: "we do anticipate some slight margin compression" due to "higher deposit costs" (Corporate Executive VP & CFO Stokes).
  • Mortgage revenue sensitivity to rates was reiterated: "given the high interest rate market" there may be limited upside "until we see a little relief" (Vice Chairman & CEO Proctor).

Final Takeaway

Ameris management emphasized that Q2 core performance and balance-sheet growth continued, while reported results absorbed an $82.5 million litigation accrual tied to a California employment verdict. Leadership maintained guidance for mid-single-digit loan and deposit growth, reiterated that deposit growth would govern loan growth, and described expected modest net interest margin compression from higher deposit costs. Management also pointed to continued capital strength and an opportunistic—but less aggressive—buyback posture, while positioning Nashville expansion as an incremental long-term organic growth opportunity.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: What's next for Goldman Sachs after its blowout Q2 earnings?
data.description:
The Goldman Sachs (GS) delivered a spectacular second quarter on July 14, 2026, blowing past expectations with record revenues of $20.34B, representing a 39.5% year-over-year growth rate, and a record GAAP EPS of $20.98, which outpaced consensus estimates by $6.44. The financial powerhouse fueled a bullish rally as capital markets activity surged, underpinned by high-profile underwriting transactions and an 11.1% increase in its quarterly dividend to $5.00 per share.

Still, despite the record-breaking quarter, market analysts debate whether this performance represents a sustainable structural upgrade to the franchise or a cyclical peak riding extraordinary macroeconomic conditions.

What Do Seeking Alpha Analysts Say About Goldman Sachs's Future?

Optimists pointed to the company's 25.5% return on tangible equity, a five-year high investment banking backlog, and structural market share gains in equities as evidence that Goldman's outperformance is durable. The firm repurchased $4B of common stock during the quarter, and management raised full-year alternatives fundraising guidance to exceed $125B. One analyst estimated that roughly 60% of the quarter's strength is structural—driven by share gains in equities, growth in asset and wealth management, and the "One Goldman Sachs" flywheel—while the remaining 40% reflects cyclical tailwinds from elevated volatility and favorable deal-making conditions.

Bearish analysts, however, highlighted the extreme cyclicality in Goldman's business despite its push into asset and wealth management. The price-to-book multiple has expanded to 3.082, a level not seen since the early 2000s, signaling frothy valuation that may leave limited upside. Balance sheet constraints, including an SLR ratio now the lowest among peers, could cap financing growth. Geopolitical volatility from Middle East tensions and the potential for a slowdown in M&A or IPO activity also present meaningful downside risks if broader markets weaken.

Here's a breakdown of what some analysts had to say:

  • Dividend Collection Agency, Rating: Buy: "After Goldman Sachs blowout quarter, I still believe shares could trend higher long-term despite near-term underperformance risks. However, investors may not want to aggressively chase the stock following its 31% rally year-to-date." - Goldman Sachs: Blowout Quarter, 11% Dividend Raise, But You Shouldn't Chase Here

  • Sandeep G. Rao, Rating: Hold: "Due to the closure of global energy supply routes, the war in the Middle East is both recessionary and inflationary and—particularly in Goldman Sachs’ case—a demonstrable detriment to one of its segments. Simultaneously, the company has effectively shifted focus towards a buildout cycle that is facing enormous public opposition on account of a variety of concerns." - Goldman Sachs Q2 2026: Strong Earnings Plus Recession Concerns Trigger Rally

  • Bay Area Ideas, Rating: Hold: "Despite stellar fundamentals, Goldman Sachs' P/B multiple of 3.082 signals frothy valuation; I maintain a cautious hold rating as risk/reward appears neutral. At this point, it seems that extraordinary fundamentals have been met by an extraordinary valuation." - Goldman Sachs Stock Is Starting To Look Frothy

  • Datalam Trading, Rating: Buy: "Valuation at 2.6x tangible book and 17.2x forward P/E is justified by sector-leading profitability and sustained AI-driven capital markets activity. You're not paying 17x for a mature business; you're paying for growth." - Goldman Sachs Just Beat Q2, Here's What It Really Means For Banks And The Economy

What Do the Quant Ratings Say?

Goldman Sachs currently carries a Hold rating of 3.45 from Seeking Alpha's quant system. Although momentum is ranked highly within its specific industry, overall valuation levels relative to historical multiples create caution. The stock ranks 5 out of 29 in the Investment Banking and Brokerage industry and 148 out of 692 in the broader Financials sector.

Profitability: Goldman Sachs earns an F grade. 23.5% ROE and 25.5% ROTE are driven by $20.34B in net revenue and robust operating leverage. The efficiency ratio improved by 6 percentage points year-over-year to 57.4%, demonstrating strong expense control relative to revenue growth.

Valuation: The stock scores poorly with a D- grade as the price-to-book ratio expanded to 3.082, representing a frothy premium far above its five-year median of around 2.16x. The forward P/E of 15.34x sits above historical averages, though some analysts argue the premium is justified by sector-leading profitability.

Growth: Goldman shows significant expansion with net earnings trending toward 38% annual growth and Global Banking & Markets revenues growing 53% year-over-year. Revenue growth of 23.32% year-over-year and EPS growth of 92% underscore the firm's momentum, with an A- grade awarded.

Momentum: The stock demonstrates notable strength, graded A, as shares surged ~29% year-to-date and roughly 61% over the past year, significantly outperforming the broader market.

What's the Latest News on Goldman Sachs?

Goldman Sachs led the historic SpaceX (SPCX) IPO, the biggest in history, and helped guide Alphabet's (GOOGL) $90B equity raise during Q2, becoming the first bank to cross $1 trillion in announced M&A volume over a six-month period. Equity underwriting revenues reached $985M, up 130% year-over-year, while investment banking fees rose 55% to $3.4B. The bank also raised $31B in private credit in Q2 alone and expanded Asset & Wealth Management inflows by $19B, marking its 34th consecutive quarter of long-term fee-based net inflows.

Goldman's investment banking growth of 55% outpaced United States competitors JPMorgan Chase (JPM) at 30%, Bank of America (BAC) at 50%, and Citigroup (C) at 44%. All major banks beat on investment banking and trading, confirming the strong capital markets environment. Equities trading revenue reached a record $7.42B, up 72% year-over-year, while FICC revenue grew 32% to $4.59B.

Recent CPI cooling from 4.2% to 3.5% paves the way for potential interest rate cuts, which could further fuel M&A and underwriting pipelines. CEO David Solomon noted the AI investment cycle is creating significant opportunities ranging from structuring and financing to risk management, with the buildout cycle still in an early stage. However, ongoing geopolitical tensions, including the re-escalation of conflict between the U.S. and Iran, continue to present uncertainty for capital markets activity in the second half of 2026.
